en.extract:
The Lesser Antillean saltator (Saltator albicollis) is a species of songbird in the tanager family Thraupidae that is found in Dominica, Martinique, Saint Kitts and Nevis, and Saint Lucia. Its natural habitats are subtropical or tropical dry forests and heavily degraded former forest.


Taxonomy
The Lesser Antillean saltator was formally described as Saltator albicollis by the French ornithologist Louis Pierre Vieillot in 1817. Vieillot erroneously reported the type locality of his specimen as Cayenne in French Guiana, but it was later corrected to Martinique. The specific epithet is derived from the Latin word meaning "white-necked" or "white-throated".
The Lesser Antillean saltator is sister to the streaked saltator, which it was previously considered to be the same species as. It has two recognised subspecies:

S. a. guadelupensis de Lafresnaye, 1844: It is found on Guadeloupe and Dominica.
S. a. albicollis Vieillot, 1817: The nominate subspecies, it is found on Martinique and Saint Lucia.


Description
Lesser Antillean saltators grow to a length of 21–22 cm (8.3–8.7 in) and a weight of 37–51.6 g (1.31–1.82 oz). They are olive-green from the back to the top of the head, with a greyish lower back and rump. The face is greyish, with a thin greenish-white supercilium. The beak is black with a yellow gape and apex. The throat is greenish-white, the chest is olive-green, and the flanks are grey. The species does not show sexual dimorphism.


Distribution and habitat
The Lesser Antillean saltator is endemic to the northern and central portions of the Lesser Antilles, where it is found on the islands of Guadeloupe, Dominica, Martinique, and Saint Lucia. It has also been recorded as a vagrant on Nevis, although it is not migratory. It inhabits a number of habitats at elevations of up to 500 m (1,600 ft), such as wet forests, swamps, mangroves, and dry forests, but is most commonly found in copses and dry forests.


Conservation
The Lesser Antillean saltator is classified as being of least concern by the IUCN as despite its small range, it is fairly common on the islands it inhabits and has a stable population.

The '''Lesser Antillean saltator''' ('''''Saltator albicollis''''') is a species of [[songbird]] in the tanager family [[Thraupidae]] that is found in [[Dominica]], [[Martinique]], [[Saint Kitts and Nevis]], and [[Saint Lucia]]. Its natural [[habitat]]s are subtropical or tropical dry [[forest]]s and heavily degraded former forest.

== Taxonomy ==
The Lesser Antillean saltator was formally described as ''Saltator albicollis'' by the French ornithologist [[Louis Pierre Vieillot]] in 1817. Vieillot erroneously reported the type locality of his specimen as [[Cayenne]] in [[French Guiana]], but it was later corrected to [[Martinique]]. The specific epithet is derived from the Latin word meaning "white-necked" or "white-throated".<ref name=":0">{{Citation |last=Brewer |first=David |title=Lesser Antillean Saltator (''Saltator albicollis'') |date=2020-03-04 |work=Birds of the World |editor-last=Billerman |editor-first=Shawn M. |url=https://birdsoftheworld.org/bow/species/leasal1/1.0/introduction |access-date=2026-05-18 |publisher=Cornell Lab of Ornithology |language=en |doi=10.2173/bow.leasal1.01 |editor2-last=Keeney |editor2-first=Brooke K. |editor3-last=Rodewald |editor3-first=Paul G. |editor4-last=Schulenberg |editor4-first=Thomas S. |url-access=subscription}}</ref>

The Lesser Antillean saltator is sister to the [[streaked saltator]], which it was previously considered to be the same species as. It has two recognised subspecies:<ref name=":0" />

* ''S. a. guadelupensis'' <small>de Lafresnaye, 1844</small>: It is found on [[Guadeloupe]] and [[Dominica]].
* ''S. a. albicollis'' <small>Vieillot, 1817</small>: The [[nominate subspecies]], it is found on Martinique and [[Saint Lucia]].

== Description ==
Lesser Antillean saltators grow to a length of {{Convert|21–22|cm|in|abbr=on}} and a weight of {{Convert|37–51.6|g|oz|abbr=on}}. They are olive-green from the back to the top of the head, with a greyish lower back and rump. The face is greyish, with a thin greenish-white supercilium. The beak is black with a yellow gape and apex. The throat is greenish-white, the chest is olive-green, and the flanks are grey. The species does not show sexual dimorphism.<ref name=":0" />

== Distribution and habitat ==
The Lesser Antillean saltator is endemic to the northern and central portions of the Lesser Antilles, where it is found on the islands of Guadeloupe, Dominica, Martinique, and Saint Lucia. It has also been recorded as a vagrant on [[Nevis]], although it is not migratory. It inhabits a number of habitats at elevations of up to {{Convert|500|m|ft|abbr=on}}, such as wet forests, swamps, mangroves, and dry forests, but is most commonly found in [[copses]] and dry forests.<ref name=":0" />

== Conservation ==
The Lesser Antillean saltator is classified as being of least concern by the IUCN as despite its small range, it is fairly common on the islands it inhabits and has a stable population.<ref name="iucn" />
